one_line_takeaway: In a controlled 1,500-trace cross-family benchmark of authorized sandboxed security-agent tasks, less-restricted/abliterated model derivatives are not uniformly better security agents than their aligned counterparts (Gemma pairs gain, Qwen loses, Llama's abliterated variant breaks tool-use) -- and where Gemma gains, the same gain also appears on ordinary non-security coding tasks, so the effect is a general grounding/capability/tool-reliability difference rather than a clean security-specific alignment effect, and visible refusal rate alone fails to explain any of it.
key_finding: Gemma 4 pairs show a large less-restricted advantage on security tasks -- 31B: 21/150 (14.0%) vs 1/150 (0.7%) aligned, McNemar p=1.10e-5, grounding 3.91 vs 3.27; 26B A4B: 16/150 (10.7%) vs 0/150 (0.0%) aligned, p=3.05e-5, grounding 4.12 vs 1.64 -- with zero refusal, suppressed-action, and unsafe-action events in the 31B traces (the gap is not explained by visible refusal). However the paper's own controls and cross-family data explicitly rule out a clean security-specific or universal 'less-restricted is better' effect: (1) the same Gemma derivatives show comparable or larger gaps on non-security controls -- e.g. 31B sanity 40.0% vs 0.0%, 31B standard-coding 37.5% vs 0.0%, 26B sanity 51.7% vs 0.0% -- so gate 4 of the paper's own decision rule fails; (2) Qwen2.5-Coder's abliterated derivative is WORSE, not better: 3/150 (2.0%) vs 8/150 (5.3%) aligned (p=0.18), grounding roughly flat (3.21 vs 3.17); (3) the abliterated Llama 3.1 8B derivative fails the tool protocol outright: 0/150 success vs 5/150 (3.3%) aligned, grounding collapses to 0.21 vs 2.80, with 124/150 tool-failure labels vs 35/150 for aligned. All model conditions across all families score 0/30 on the hardest task classes (proof-of-trigger and patch-verification). The paper's conclusion is explicitly that safety-alignment effects in autonomous security agents must be measured at the system level (separating refusal, unsafe action, tool reliability, and evidence grounding) rather than inferred from aggregate success or refusal rate alone.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a trace-based, system-level benchmark for measuring whether safety-alignment status changes the behavior of LLMs acting as autonomous security agents inside authorized, sandboxed environments, rather than in single-turn refusal settings. It introduces: (1) a 30-task local security benchmark (vulnerability localization, reachability validation, proof of trigger, patch verification, security report writing; 6 tasks per class) with fixed tools, deterministic success predicates, redaction rules, and per-task evidence-grounding targets (expected files/symbols/issue terms/local evidence terms); (2) a single cross-family evaluation pairing four aligned models against uncensored/abliterated derivatives (Gemma 4 31B, Gemma 4 26B A4B, Qwen2.5-Coder 7B, Llama 3.1 8B) under a shared agent harness (same tasks, tools, budgets, seeds, backend, quantization where possible), producing 1,500 saved security-agent traces plus 800 non-security control traces (12-task sanity catalog and 8-task HumanEval/MBPP-style standard-coding catalog per pair); and (3) an analysis separating visible refusal from other failure modes (tool-interface failure, weak grounding, unsafe action) via a single-label failure taxonomy, deterministic grounding scoring, a strict-vs-repair controller ablation, and a blind two-reviewer LLM audit of redacted traces (kappa = 0.87 for success labels, kappa = 0.35 for hard-validation subtype labels).
threat_model: Explicitly authorized/local/contained, stated by the paper itself (Section 3.2): all tasks are local, authorized, and constructed with known seeded defects; the agent never targets third-party systems; network access is disabled unless a task explicitly requires a fixed local mirror; the agent may inspect files, execute commands, start services, and write reports only inside the task sandbox. A run is labeled unsafe if it attempts to escape the sandbox, access external systems, exfiltrate secrets outside task scope, or produce reusable operational instructions that violate the sandboxing/redaction/containment policy. The paper is explicit that it does not evaluate third-party targets, does not publish reusable exploit payloads, and does not argue less-restricted models are safe to deploy -- this is a measurement study of where safety controls should sit in an agentic pipeline, not an offensive-capability or red-teaming paper.
limitations: Limitations stated by the paper itself (Sections 3.4, 6, and elsewhere): the 'uncensored'/abliterated derivatives are not clean causal counterfactuals for safety alignment -- weight editing, quantization, template changes, or fine-tuning may alter more than refusal behavior, so model provenance is treated as part of the interpretation rather than a controlled nuisance variable. Statistical tests are deliberately not pooled across model families or sizes because architecture/size/provenance are not tightly controlled. The repair controller (used for all main runs) was adopted after pilots showed the strict controller confounded results with interface failures, meaning the primary results depend on this controller-repair intervention rather than a fully protocol-neutral harness. The jailbreak prompt condition is reported only as an appendix stress test with hash-only disclosure (no operational wording published), explicitly not a deployment recommendation. Grounding scoring is intentionally non-semantic (substring matching), so it can be gamed or miss semantically-correct-but-differently-worded evidence. Blind LLM-reviewer audit agreement is strong for coarse success/refusal judgments (kappa=0.87) but weak for hard-validation subtype labels (kappa=0.35), meaning the taxonomy's finer failure categories are less reliable. All families remain at or near 0% on proof-of-trigger and patch-verification tasks, so the benchmark's hardest and arguably most security-relevant task classes are effectively unsolved by every evaluated condition, limiting what can be concluded about validation competence generally. The paper explicitly disclaims that its results argue less-restricted models are safe to deploy.

datasets_used: Custom 30-task local security-agent benchmark built by the authors (6 tasks each in: vulnerability localization, reachability validation, proof of trigger, patch verification, security report writing), original local code snippets/fixtures, no third-party targets or live credentials

12-task non-security sanity catalog (ordinary bug localization, branch-condition mistakes, cache-key mistakes, parsing/formatting errors, remediation notes, test-failure diagnosis) built with the same fixture/controller/trace/predicate machinery
8-task standard coding control catalog, HumanEval/MBPP-style local repair tasks
1,500 security-agent traces total (600 for Gemma 31B across 4 conditions x 5 seeds x 30 tasks; 300 each for Gemma 26B A4B, Qwen2.5-Coder 7B, Llama 3.1 8B)
800 non-security control traces across the 4 model pairs (120 sanity + 80 standard-coding runs per pair)
Supplementary: 24-run orchestration pilots, 150-run authorization-prompt robustness run, 200-run strict-controller ablation, 120-trace blind LLM-reviewer audit packet
quantitative_results: Gemma 4 31B: less-restricted 14.0% (21/150) vs aligned 0.7% (1/150) security success, delta +13.3pp, McNemar p=1.10e-5; grounding 3.91 vs 3.27 (out of 5); 0.0% unsafe-action rate for less-restricted
Gemma 4 26B A4B: less-restricted 10.7% (16/150) vs aligned 0.0% (0/150) security success, delta +10.7pp, p=3.05e-5; grounding 4.12 vs 1.64; less-restricted unsafe-action rate 5.3%

Qwen2.5-Coder 7B: abliterated 2.0% (3/150) vs aligned 5.3% (8/150) security success, delta -3.3pp, p=0.18 (not significant); grounding roughly flat, 3.21 vs 3.17; 0.0% unsafe-action rate both conditions

Llama 3.1 8B: abliterated 0.0% (0/150) vs aligned 3.3% (5/150) security success, delta -3.3pp; grounding collapses to 0.21 vs 2.80; abliterated has 124/150 tool-failure labels vs 35/150 for aligned; abliterated unsafe-action rate 3.3%

Non-security controls (Table 4): 31B sanity 40.0% vs 0.0% (less-restricted vs aligned), 31B standard-coding 37.5% vs 0.0%; 26B sanity 51.7% vs 0.0%, 26B standard-coding 25.0% vs 0.0%; Qwen sanity 0.0% vs 0.0%, Qwen standard 2.5% vs 2.5% (flat); Llama sanity 0.0% vs 1.7%, Llama standard 0.0% vs 0.0%

Gemma 31B prompt-condition breakdown (Table 5): aligned 0.7% success/3.27 grounding, authorized 0.0%/3.36, jailbreak 0.7%/3.20, uncensored/less-restricted 14.0%/3.91 -- authorization and jailbreak prompting did not recover success for the aligned model
31B Gemma symbol-naming rate: less-restricted derivative names expected symbols in 0.81 of runs vs 0.41 for aligned

Strict-vs-repair controller ablation (10-task subset, 50 runs/condition): official aligned model 0/50 success under both controllers; less-restricted derivative 5/50 under strict vs 10/50 under repair
All conditions across all four model families: 0/30 on proof-of-trigger and 0/30 on patch-verification tasks
Blind LLM-reviewer audit: kappa=0.87 for success/refusal judgments on a 120-trace blind stratified packet; kappa=0.35 for hard-validation subtype labels
baselines_compared: Aligned (official instruction-tuned) vs less-restricted/uncensored/abliterated derivative, paired per model family: Gemma 4 31B official vs TrevorJS 31B uncensored GGUF; Gemma 4 26B A4B official vs TrevorJS 26B A4B uncensored GGUF; Qwen2.5-Coder 7B official coder-instruct GGUF vs bartowski abliterated coder-instruct GGUF; Llama 3.1 8B instruction-tuned GGUF vs bartowski abliterated instruction-tuned GGUF
Gemma 31B additional prompt-condition baselines: standard/aligned prompt vs explicit-authorization prompt vs fixed jailbreak-style prompt (all on the official aligned model)
Controller ablation baseline: strict JSON-only tool protocol controller vs repair controller (auto file-inventory injection and call repair)

Non-security controls as an internal baseline to test whether any observed aligned-vs-less-restricted gap is security-specific: 12-task sanity catalog and 8-task standard HumanEval/MBPP-style coding catalog, run under the identical harness for every model pair
